fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a serialization utility that can look at our data types and automatically figure out what the wire format should look like — basically no more hand-writing schemas every time someone adds a field. It should spit out something machine-readable (JSON-based) that describes the shape of the data, and also be able to turn actual values into a compact byte sequence and read them back. Think of it like what we did with the nullable handling in the profile service last quarter — same kind of 'wrap it in a union' pattern.

The tricky bits: integers have some collapsing behavior (the narrower ones fold into a single wire type), characters are special-cased with a marker, and text needs a length prefix baked in. For enums specifically, we want the schema to carry documentation, aliases, and a designated fallback symbol — and if someone misconfigures the fallback (more than one), it should fail loudly with a recognizable error token, not a stack trace. We also need the decode side to be tolerant of schema drift, so if the reader's enum is a subset of the writer's, unknown symbols resolve gracefully. Output bytes should be lowercase hex. Please keep the I/O wiring totally separate from the core logic — last time everything got tangled and it was a nightmare to test.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fun deriveSchema(kind: String, nullable: Boolean = false): SchemaNode

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 8-bit, 16-bit, and 32-bit signed integer widths all collapse to the single wire type 'int'. Only the 64-bit width maps to 'long'. So byte, short, and int all produce the schema string "int".

trigger_keywords: what does char look like in the schema
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A character is represented as a 32-bit integer type carrying a logicalType marker. The schema is a JSON object: {"type": "int", "logicalType": "char"}, printed as a pretty multi-line JSON object — not a bare quoted string like the other primitives.
An optional (nullable) value produces a two-branch union where null comes first. The output is a JSON array: [ "null", <underlying-type-schema> ]. The null branch must always be the first element.

trigger_keywords: how are integers encoded on the wire
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All signed integer widths (8, 16, 32, 64-bit) and character code points use zig-zag variable-length integer encoding. For example, the integer 44 encodes as the single byte 0x58 (zig-zag of 44 = 88 = 0x58).

A string encodes as the zig-zag variable-length integer of its UTF-8 byte length, immediately followed by the raw UTF-8 bytes. For example 'Hello world' (11 bytes) encodes as 0x16 (zig-zag of 11) followed by the 11 UTF-8 bytes.
Single-precision floats encode as exactly 4 bytes in little-endian IEEE 754 format. Double-precision floats encode as exactly 8 bytes in little-endian IEEE 754 format. No length prefix — fixed width.

trigger_keywords: what goes in an enum schema
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A valid enum schema is a JSON object with: "type": "enum", "name" (the type name), "doc" (documentation string), "symbols" (ordered array of symbol names), "default" (the single fallback symbol name), and "aliases" (array of alias strings). All these fields must appear in that order in the output.

trigger_keywords: what happens when two symbols are default
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If an enum declaration marks more than one symbol as the fallback default, schema derivation must fail. The adapter must print exactly the string 'error=multiple_enum_defaults' followed by a newline. No exception class names, no stack traces — just that neutral error token.

trigger_keywords: how are record bytes laid out
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A record adds zero framing bytes of its own. Its encoding is the strict concatenation of each field's encoded value in declared field order, using the exact same per-type encoding rules as standalone scalars. There are no length prefixes, no tag bytes, no delimiters between fields.

When decoding with schema evolution, the writer uses a wider enum (knows symbol 'A' and 'B') and the reader uses a narrower enum (knows only 'A', with 'UNKNOWN' as its default). If the written symbol exists in the reader's set it decodes to itself. If not, it decodes to the reader's designated default symbol — 'UNKNOWN' — without throwing an error.

trigger_keywords: when is schema printed as quoted string vs object
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Primitive schemas that resolve to a single type name are printed as a bare quoted string (e.g., "int" or "string"). Only schemas with additional structure — like the char logical-type object or a record/enum object — are printed as a pretty-printed multi-line JSON object. The nullable union is printed as a JSON array.
A boolean encodes to exactly one byte: 0x01 for true and 0x00 for false. It does not use zig-zag encoding.

The core business logic (schema derivation, encoding, decoding) must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. A separate execution adapter is solely responsible for reading a JSON request from stdin, calling into the core domain, and formatting the result to stdout. The core must never directly reference I/O.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'schema', 'encode', 'decode_enum_evolution'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same kind of 'wrap it in a union' pattern we did with the nullable handling in the profile service last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2: nullable primitive schema derivation — the schema is a two-branch union array [ "null", <primitive-schema> ] where null is always the first branch, as specified in feature2_nullable_primitive_schema.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the collapsing behavior where narrower ones fold into a single wire type
context_pointers.actual_meaning: int8 (byte), int16 (short), and int32 (int) all map to the Avro wire type 'int'; only int64 maps to 'long' — defined in Feature 1 and exercised in feature1_primitive_schema.json and feature6_record_encoding.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: fail loudly with a recognizable error token, not a stack trace
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The adapter must print exactly 'error=multiple_enum_defaults\n' when an enum has more than one default symbol, as specified in feature4_enum_schema.json; no Java/Kotlin exception details may appear

context_pointers.vague_reference: decode side to be tolerant of schema drift so unknown symbols resolve gracefully
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7 enum decode evolution: writer enum has symbols {A, B}, reader enum has symbols {A} with default 'UNKNOWN'; symbol 'B' written by the writer resolves to 'UNKNOWN' when read by the narrower reader, as specified in feature7_enum_decode_default.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference with the feature descriptions in start.md. The test cases are the authoritative contract for exact output formatting.

feature1_primitive_schema.json.description: Generate the serialization schema for a single scalar value of a given primitive category. The request names a primitive kind (logical boolean, the four signed integer widths, the two floating-point widths, text, and a single character). The adapter resolves the schema for that scalar type and prints its canonical schema document. Narrow integer widths collapse onto the 32-bit integer type, the character type is represented as a 32-bit integer carrying a logical-type marker, and the remaining kinds map to their direct wire types.

feature2_nullable_primitive_schema.json.description: Generate the serialization schema for an optional (nullable) scalar value of a given primitive category. The request names the same primitive kinds as the non-optional case but marks the value as optional. The resulting schema is a two-branch union of the null type and the underlying primitive's schema, in that order, so a missing value can be represented on the wire.

feature3_record_schema.json.description: Generate the serialization schema for a structured record type. The request names a record shape: a flat record whose fields cover every primitive category, a record that embeds another record as one of its fields, and a record nested several levels deep where each level holds the next level as its only field. The adapter prints the canonical record schema document, with each field rendered as a name plus the schema of its type, and embedded records expanded inline at their point of use.

feature4_enum_schema.json.description: Generate the serialization schema for an enumerated type, and reject an invalid one. A valid enumeration declares an ordered set of symbols, may carry an alias and a documentation string, and designates one symbol as the fallback default; its schema lists the symbols in declaration order and records the alias, documentation and default. If a type declares more than one fallback default symbol it is invalid and schema generation fails; the adapter then emits a neutral error line identifying the failure category instead of a schema.

feature5_primitive_encoding.json.description: Encode a single scalar value into the compact binary wire format and print the resulting bytes as a lowercase hexadecimal string. The request names the primitive category and supplies the value. Booleans encode to a single byte; the signed integer widths and the character (by its code point) use zig-zag variable-length integers; the floating-point widths use fixed little-endian layouts; text encodes as its zig-zag length prefix followed by its UTF-8 bytes.

feature6_record_encoding.json.description: Encode a structured record value into the compact binary wire format and print the resulting bytes as a lowercase hexadecimal string. The request names a record shape and supplies the field values as an object keyed by field name. Records carry no framing of their own: the encoding is simply the concatenation of each field's encoded value in declared field order, using the same per-type encodings as for standalone scalars.

feature7_enum_decode_default.json.description: Decode an enumerated value using a reader type whose symbol set differs from the writer's. A value is first written with a wider enumeration (one that knows the given symbol) and then read back with a narrower enumeration that shares the same enum name and default symbol. When the written symbol also exists in the reader, it decodes to itself; when the written symbol is unknown to the reader, decoding falls back to the reader's designated default symbol rather than failing. The adapter prints the decoded symbol's name.
